steps.title: Connect one intersection to tangency
steps.content:
The line and parabola have exactly one intersection only when the line just touches the parabola. Algebraically, that means setting their $y$-values equal should produce a quadratic with one repeated solution:
$$2x^2-13x+24=3x+a$$
$$2x^2-16x+(24-a)=0$$
A quadratic has one repeated solution when its discriminant is $0$.

steps.title: Graph the corrected line
steps.content:
Set the discriminant equal to $0$:
$$(-16)^2-4(2)(24-a)=0$$
$$256-192+8a=0$$
$$64+8a=0$$
$$a=-8$$ So the line is $y=3x-8$. Graph $y=2x^2-13x+24$ with $y=3x-8$ as a visual check; the graphs touch once.

steps.title: Confirm the x-value
steps.content:
Substitute $a=-8$ into the intersection equation:
$$2x^2-16x+32=0$$
Divide by $2$: $$x^2-8x+16=0$$
$$(x-4)^2=0$$
So the single intersection has $x=4$. That matches choice C, so C is correct.
